spans.content: Auto-Prompting SAM for Weakly Supervised
Landslide Extraction
Jian Wang, Xiaokang Zhang , Senior Member, IEEE, Xianping
\mathrm { M a } ^ { \textregistered }
, Graduate Student Member, IEEE,
Weikang
\mathrm { Y u } ^ { \textregistered }
, Graduate Student Member, IEEE, and Pedram Ghamisi , Senior Member, IEEE
Abstract— Weakly supervised landslide extraction aims to
identify landslide regions from remote sensing data using
models trained with weak labels, particularly image-level labels.
However, it is often challenged by the imprecise boundaries of
the extracted objects due to the lack of pixel-wise supervision
and the properties of landslide objects. To tackle these issues,
we propose a simple, yet effective method by auto-prompting
the Segment Anything Model (SAM), that is, APSAM. Instead
of depending on high-quality class activation maps (CAMs) for
pseudo-labeling or fine-tuning SAM, our method directly yields
fine-grained segmentation masks from SAM inference through
prompt engineering. Specifically, it adaptively generates hybrid
prompts from the CAMs obtained by an object localization
network. To provide sufficient information for SAM prompting,
an adaptive prompt generation (APG) algorithm is designed to
fully leverage the visual patterns of CAMs, enabling the effi-
cient generation of pseudo-masks for landslide extraction. These
informative prompts can identify the extent of landslide areas
(box prompts) and denote the centers of landslide objects (point
prompts), guiding SAM in landslide segmentation. Experimental
results on high-resolution aerial and satellite datasets demon-
strate the effectiveness of our method, achieving improvements
of at least
in
{ \pmb F } { \pmb 1 }
-score and
in IoU compared to other
state-of-the-art methods. The source codes and datasets will be
available at https://github.com/zxk688
Index Terms— Landslide extraction, object localization,
prompt, segment anything model (SAM), weak supervision.
I. INTRODUCTION
L ANDSLIDES, one of the most common natural hazardsworldwide, cause significant economic damage and result
ANDSLIDES,one of the most common natural hazards
in numerous human casualties each year. Accurate recording
of landslide characteristics, such as size, location, and spatial
extent, is crucial for hazard and risk assessment. Landslide
extraction from high-resolution remote sensing imagery has

(Corresponding author: Xiaokang Zhang.)

Science and Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology,
Wuhan 430081, China (e-mail: natezhangxk@gmail.com).
Xianping Ma is with the School of Science and Engineering, The
Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen 518172, China (e-mail:
xianpingma@link.cuhk.edu.cn).
Weikang Yu is with the Chair of Data Science in Earth Observation,
Technical University of Munich (TUM), 80333 Munich, Germany, and also
with the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf, Freiberg, 09599 Dresden,
Germany (e-mail: w.yu@hzdr.de).
Pedram Ghamisi is with the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf,
Freiberg, 09599 Dresden, Germany, and also with the Lancaster Environ-
ment Centre, Lancaster University, LA1 4YR Lancaster, U.K. (e-mail:
p.ghamisi@hzdr.de).
Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/LGRS.2025.3567600
gained increasing attention with advancements in remote
sensing technology [1]. Recently, deep learning techniques
have demonstrated their effectiveness in pixel-wise landslide
extraction from high-resolution imagery [2]. However, the
high performance of landslide extraction models relies heavily
on large volumes of training data. The manual annotation
process incurs substantial costs, and both unlabeled data and
image-level labeled data are relatively easy to acquire [3].
Therefore, recent studies have investigated weakly supervised
learning to address this issue, which utilizes image-level labels
for object extraction. However, such supervision only indicates
the presence of a specific object category without providing
information about the object’s spectral or spatial character-
istics. Existing weakly supervised methods have leveraged
class activation maps (CAMs) to generate pseudo-masks [4].
They focus on enhancing CAM quality by capturing more
complete regions through local–global semantic contexts [5]
or pixel affinity relationships [6]. Moreover, they rely on
multistage training with multiple networks and require post-
processing techniques, such as dense random fields, to refine
pseudo-labels [7]. As a result, they struggle to maintain flexi-
bility and stability when handling remote sensing images with
complex scenes, and the generated pseudo-labels still suffer
from spatial fragmentation and poor boundary delineation for
landslide extraction tasks.
Recently, the segment anything model (SAM) [8] has been
introduced into weakly supervised learning to leverage the
instance segmentation capability of SAM [9], [10]. Since SAM
is pretrained in a class-agnostic manner, its zero-shot segmen-
tation performance in remote sensing heavily relies on manual
prompts as guidance [11], which are unavailable. Compared
to fine-tuning SAM encoder or introducing learnable prompt
embeddings in fully supervised learning [12], [13], the chal-
lenges in weakly supervised learning include the lack of
fine-grained supervision to guide or fine-tune SAM, as well
as the ambiguity of automatically generated prompts, which
often fail to produce satisfactory results. Therefore, SAM has
primarily been used as a postprocessing tool or a feature
extractor for pseudo-label modeling after CAM-based meth-
ods were exploited to generate initial pseudo-labels [9], [10].
However, these methods mainly focus on segmenting objects
with well-defined edges, such as buildings and urban scenes.
Due to the complex background interference and weak
object edges in landslide-related images, SAM’s capabili-
ties of segmentation and boundary refinement significantly
degrade.

Fig. 1. Overview of the proposed APSAM for landslide extraction.
To address the abovementioned issues, APSAM, a weakly
supervised landslide extraction method by auto-prompting
SAM, is proposed. Instead of adapting SAM for feature
extraction or using manual prompts [12], we utilized the
object localization capability of CAM-based methods with
image-level supervision, which can guide SAM in producing
fine-grained segmentation masks. Specifically, it automatically
explores hybrid prompts from an object localization network.
These prompts can identify the extent of landslide areas
(box prompts) and represent landslide objects (point prompts),
guiding SAM in landslide segmentation. To provide sufficient
information for SAM, an adaptive prompt generation (APG)
algorithm is designed to exploit the visual patterns of CAMs.
Compared to previous methods, APSAM is less sensitive to
CAMs, as it generates prompts by capturing spatial structures
and distributions of landslide objects, rather than directly
obtaining pseudo-masks through hard thresholding of the
CAM. Moreover, it effectively mitigates the impact of CAM’s
boundary ambiguity by leveraging SAM’s boundary-aware and
prompt-driven segmentation capability. As a result, reliable
pseudo-masks for landslide extraction can be obtained by
leveraging these automatically generated prompts.
II. METHODOLOGY
This section provides a detailed description of APSAM,
as illustrated in Fig. 1. It consists of three stages. First,
an object-localization network is utilized to generate CAMs.
Then, an APG algorithm is applied to automatically create
informative point and box prompts. After that, the pretrained
SAM is employed to extract landslide pseudo-masks based on
the generated prompts.
A. Object Localization
To coarsely locate the landslides and obtain initial seed
regions, we train an object localization network [4] using
image-level supervision. Specifically, an image classification
network is employed with a ResNet50 backbone, followed by a
global average pooling (GAP) layer and a classifier to generate
image-level predictions. Given the training data
with
N
samples, where I M is the input image and
represents the image-level label (0 and 1 mean the background
and landslide, respectively). The network is trained using a
binary cross-entropy loss between the predicted logits and
labels. After that, class activations C AM can be obtained by
performing inference on the trained network as follows:
\boldsymbol {C A M} _ {i, j} (x, y) = \frac {\theta_ {j} ^ {\mathrm {T}} \boldsymbol {F} _ {i} (x , y)}{\max  _ {x , y} \theta_ {j} ^ {\mathrm {T}} \boldsymbol {F} _ {i} (x , y)} \tag {1}
where
( x , y )
indexes the spatial location in the input image,
\theta _ { j }
denotes the classification weights of the
j
th category,
and
F _ { i }
represents the feature map embedded by the backbone.
Notably, although the activation map can focus on the most
discriminative parts of the feature map, it typically only
provides coarse object localization without precise boundaries
of objects.
B. Adaptive Prompt Generation
We propose automatically generating point- and box-level
prompts from CAMs to mitigate errors caused by uneven and
coarse activations. The implementation of the proposed APG
algorithm is illustrated as follows.
1) Binarization: First, the binarization on the generated
heatmap is performed to extract the regions of interest. For
each pixel, if its grayscale value exceeds the threshold
T \in
[0, 255], that pixel is marked as foreground in the binary
image. Otherwise, it is marked as background. Let
H ( x , y )
represent the grayscale value of the pixel
( x , y )
in the heatmap
C A M \in \mathbb { R } ^ { H \times W }
. The binarization can be expressed as follows:
if
H ( x , y )
is greater than the threshold
T
, then the binary
image
\pmb { { \cal B } } ( x , y )
takes the value 1; otherwise,
\pmb { { \cal B } } ( x , y )
takes the
value 0.
2) Counters Extraction: CAMs can be approximated as a
smooth continuous surface, where high-activated regions cor-
respond to elevated parts [4]. By setting a threshold, which is
similar to a contour line slicing through this surface, the result-
ing contours form closed boundaries. Once the specific binary
regions are obtained, the contours in the binary image can be
detected. After that, a list of contours
along with hierarchy information can be obtained, where
K
is

c _ { k }
denotes the kth contour in the
contour set
c
. Then, the minimum bounding rectangle can be
generated for each detected closed contour.

dinates
of

can be obtained, by drawing
a rectangle around each detected contour, where

calculate various characteristics
m _ {p, q} = \sum_ {x} \sum_ {y} x ^ {p} y ^ {q} c _ {k} (x, y) \tag {2}

{ \pmb p } _ { k }

C. Pseudo-Labeling With SAM
SAM incorporates a Vision Transformer-based image
encoder, a lightweight mask decoder, and a versatile prompt
encoder that can accept inputs such as points, bounding boxes,
masks, and text [8]. The variety of prompt methods allows
users to focus on different scenes and objects, providing
SAM with strong flexibility for diverse applications. Following
SAM,
\mathbf { \nabla } _ { P _ { i } }
is encoded by combining its positional encod-
ing with a learned embedding that distinguishes foreground
from background. Similarly,
\pmb { B } _ { i }
is represented through two
components. The first is the positional encoding of its top-
left corner, augmented with a learned embedding specific to
this position, while the second is the positional encoding
of its bottom-right corner, paired with a learned embedding
corresponding to that location. Finally, SAM’s image encoder
and prompt encoder separately generate image features and
sparse prompt embeddings, which are then input into SAM’s
mask decoder to produce accurate pseudo-labels. The process
can be represented as follows:

\hat {S} _ {i} = \mathcal {D} _ {\text {m a s k}} \left(\mathcal {E} _ {\text {i m g}} \left(I _ {i}\right), \mathcal {E} _ {\text {p r o m p t}} \left(\left[ P _ {i}, B _ {i} \right]\right)\right) \tag {4}
where
{ \pmb { \mathcal { E } } } _ { \mathrm { i m g } }
denotes SAM’s image encoder and
\mathcal { D } _ { \mathrm { m a s k } }
repre-
sents SAM’s mask decoder. Moreover,
\hat { S } _ { i }
is the pseudo-label
generated by SAM.
III. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS
A. Data Description
Two high-resolution datasets, Hong Kong and Turkey, are
used to evaluate the proposed method [2]. The Hong Kong
dataset contains images captured in 2014, with a spatial resolu-
tion of
0 . 5 \mathrm { m }
, consisting of aerial images. The TurKey dataset
contains images captured in 2017, with a spatial resolution of
0 . 5 9 \mathrm { m }
, consisting of Google Earth images, covering landslides
caused by heavy rain in parts of Turkey. Both datasets have
their images cropped to a size of
, with a
overlap between adjacent images. The Hong Kong dataset
uses 630 images for training with image-level labels and tests
with pixel-level labels. The TurKey dataset uses 1590 images
for training with image-level labels and tests with pixel-level
labels.
B. Experimental Setup
In the object localization section, original CAM [4] is
employed to generate heatmaps. Furthermore, the SGD opti-
mizer is chosen with a weight decay of 0.0001 and a
momentum of 0.9 to train the network. The learning rate
was initially set to 0.1 with a step-wise decay scheduler
applied. In the prompts generation section, the threshold
T
is
set to 120. The third step utilizes SAM’s powerful prompt seg-
mentation capabilities to generate pseudo-labels. Here, we use
the huge version of SAM. Finally, to ensure fairness in the
Fig. 2. Landslide extraction results on the Hong Kong dataset. (a) Original

experiment, pseudo-labels generated by various methods are
trained on ResUNet to obtain the final results. The proposed
approach was compared with several state-of-the-art weakly
supervised methods, such as context decoupling augmenta-
tion (CDA) [14], complementary patch network (CPN) [15],
class-specific erasing method (CSE) [16], feature-level flipping
CAM (FilpCAM) [17], from-SAM-to-CAMs (S2C) [18], and
local–global anchor guidance network (LGAGNet) [5]. For
quantitative evaluation, five commonly used metrics were
employed: overall accuracy (OA), precision, recall,
F 1
-score,
and IoU.
C. Results
As shown in Fig. 2, the extraction results of various
irregular-shaped landslides in the Hong Kong dataset are
presented. It can be observed that due to the different learning
abilities of various weakly supervised methods for landslides
of different shapes, some methods struggle to detect and
generate reliable landslide regions, such as CDA and CPN,
where large areas of undetected regions exist in some areas.
In comparison to other methods, our method achieves more
precise boundaries and performs well even in cases of hollow
areas, which are difficult to predict. From Table I, we can
see that while CDA and LGAGNet excel in precision and
recall, they fall short in
-score and IoU, especially CDA,
which has a relatively low IoU. This means that although
these methods result in fewer misclassified landslide regions,
they perform poorly in overall segmentation accuracy and the
overlap of target regions. Our network can better preserve
detailed information on the landslide objects and achieves the
highest OA,
F 1
-score, and IoU values.
As shown in Fig. 3, the extraction results of various irregu-
larly shaped landslides from the Turkey dataset are presented.
It can be observed that due to the varying learning capa-
bilities of different weakly supervised methods for different

we conducted ablation experiments on the Hong Kong dataset
as shown in Table II. The results indicate that the combination

formance in the landslide extraction task with the highest
OA and IoU, demonstrating the effectiveness of both point and
box prompts. When only using box prompts, the recall slightly
increases to
, indicating that box-based prompts can
more completely locate target areas. In contrast, using only
points as prompts leads to a more significant degradation
in performance. Furthermore, we compared a fixed parame-
ter
T
with the automatic threshold selection using the Otsu
algorithm for image binarization. While the performance of
Otsu is acceptable, better performance is achieved with man-
ually set thresholds in the range of 50–160, where the IoU
values remain stable and exceed
, as shown in Fig. 4.
In the re-training process with pseudo-labels, SegFormer,
a more advanced semantic segmentation network, slightly
outperforms the adopted ResUNet-50 across all evaluation
metrics. This also demonstrates that our approach is not
dependent on a specific backbone or network.
As shown in Table III, compared with previous methods,
such as LGAGNet, which use a ResNet50 backbone to gen-
erate pseudo-labels, APSAM with a SAM-Huge backbone
shows higher parameter counts and GPU memory usage.
However, using SAM2 significantly reduces computational

costs and model complexity. Notably, all SAM backbones
achieve lower inference times than LGAGNet because it
requires additional structure-aware object locating modules
and global anchor aggregation operations to enhance feature
representation. In comparison, the pseudo-labeling by the
SAM series utilizes lightweight prompt encoders and enables
one-step segmentation outputs. More importantly, our APSAM
can fully exploit the SAM’s segmentation capability, resulting
in better segmentation performance.
From visual comparison shown in Fig. 5, it is evident that
SAM can automatically create a grid of point prompts over
the image for full-image segmentation; however, it is hindered
by complex backgrounds and nonlandslide areas, making it
unable to accurately locate and segment the landslide regions.
In contrast, APSAM generates more accurate point and box
prompts through CAMs, resulting in better segmentation of
the landslide region. For low-activated regions or blurred
activations, such as some regions of the first and second rows
in Fig. 5(e), APSAM still generates hybrid prompts that cover
and highlight the essential parts of object contours, making it
less sensitive to the quality of CAMs.
IV. CONCLUSION
This study presents a novel weakly supervised landslide
extraction network based on the SAM, named APSAM.
Instead of focusing on generating precise CAMs for pixel-
wise pseudo-labeling, our approach attempts to leverage an
APG algorithm to obtain accurate prompts from CAMs.
APSAM effectively addresses challenges posed by irregular
shapes, fragmented boundaries, and uncertain localization of
landslide objects by exploiting robust and hybrid prompts
and unleashing SAM’s strong prompt-driven segmentation
capability Experiments on two high-resolution remote sensing
datasets show that APSAM outperforms other state-of-the-art
weakly supervised methods. Future work will focus on end-to-
end training by combining the localization network with SAM.
Specifically, SAM can aid the localization network in learning
spatial patterns more effectively, while the activations from the
localization network can be utilized to prompt SAM.
